XRD patterns of the synthesized α-MnSe samples with different starting raw materials at 180 °C for 36 h (a: MnSe-1; b: MnSe-2; c: MnSe-3; d: MnSe-4; e: MnSe-5; f: MnSe-6; g: MnSe-7; h: MnSe-8).](figure1.png) In order to further investigate the effect of the starting raw materials on the crystallinity of the resultant α-MnSe, the alcohol types and the SDS concentrations were varied (Fig. 1e–h). As shown in Fig. 1e, in the ethanol/water solution containing 2 g of SDS (MnSe-5), except for the characteristic diffraction peaks of α-MnSe, some other diffraction peaks appeared. We attempted, but failed to index these peak. This impurity may be a type of Mn complex produced under the given hydrothermal condition. When glycerol was replaced by methanol (MnSe-6), pure phase α-MnSe could be obtained (Fig. 1f). Moreover, the peak intensity of the MnSe-5 and MnSe-6 samples is stronger than that of the MnSe-4 sample, suggesting that glycerol is a more suitable assistant agent to obtain α-MnSe with low crystallinity compared with ethanol and methanol. In the glycerol/water solution with 0.5 g (MnSe-7) and 5 g of SDS (MnSe-8), pure phase α-MnSe could be obtained (Fig. 1g and h). Moreover, Fig. 1 shows that the peak intensity of the MnSe-7 sample (Fig. 1g) is stronger than that of the MnSe-4 and MnSe-8 samples (Fig. 1d and h), suggesting that the increasing of the SDS concentration can help reduce crystallinity degree of the synthesized α-MnSe crystals. ![Fig. 2. SEM images of the synthesized α-MnSe samples with different starting raw materials at 180 °C for 36 h (a: MnSe-1; b: MnSe-2; c: MnSe-3; d: MnSe-4; e: MnSe-5; f: MnSe-6; g: MnSe-7; h: MnSe-8).](figure2.png) As mentioned above, in the alcohol/water solution with SDS, the synergistic effect of glycerol and high concentration SDS is the key to obtain α-MnSe with low crystallinity. SEM was used to further investigate the effect of the starting raw materials on the morphology of the resultant α-MnSe and the results are shown in Fig. 2. As shown in Fig. 2a, the MnSe-1 sample had polyhedral shapes and the average size was found to be approximately 12 μm. The average size of the MnSe-2 sample decreased to be approximately 8 μm with SDS. When glycerol was used, the average particle size further decreased (Fig. 2c). In the glycerol/water solution with 2 g of SDS, α-MnSe crystals with particle size distribution between 1 and 4 μm could be synthesized (Fig. 2d). As glycerol was replaced by ethanol (Fig. 2e), the MnSe-5 sample with polyhedral shape could be obtained and the morphology became similar to that of the MnSe-1 sample (Fig. 2a), while in the presence of methanol (Fig. 2f), the synthesized α-MnSe crystals (MnSe-6) had polyhedral shapes and the particle size distribution became narrower than that of the MnSe-5 sample (Fig. 2e). Moreover, the surface and the particle size of the MnSe-5 and MnSe-6 samples became smoother and larger than that of the MnSe-4 sample, respectively. These results indicate that the use of glycerol can help form rough surface and reduce the particle size. As shown in Fig. 2g, in the glycerol/water solution containing low concentration SDS (0.5 g), α-MnSe crystals (MnSe-7) with irregular shape and serious agglomeration can be obtained. In comparison, in the mixed solution containing high concentration SDS (5 g), α-MnSe crystals (MnSe-8) with special crooked belt shape could be obtained (Fig. 2h). These results suggest that the morphology of α-MnSe crystals can be easily tailored by adjusting the concentration of SDS and high concentration SDS facilitates the formation of special belt crystals. Moreover, the particle size of the MnSe-4 sample is also smaller than that of the MnSe-7 and MnSe-8 samples. The EDS results demonstrated that the molar ratios of Mn/Se of the synthesized α-MnSe crystals were close to 1. This ratio was consistent with the stoichiometric composition of α-MnSe. These results are in good agreement with the XRD results (Fig. 1). To investigate the effect of the time evolution, we varied the hydrothermal time from 12 h to 48 h with glycerol and 2 g of SDS. Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of the resultant samples synthesized at different times. As shown in Fig. 3a, after 12 h reaction, the as-prepared MnSe-9 sample was composed of two phases: MnSe2 (JCPDS 20-0722) and α-MnSe. Fig. 3b–d shows that α-MnSe became the main product with the elongation of the reaction time. After 36 h reaction (MnSe-4), pure phase α-MnSe could be produced and no reflection peaks of MnSe2 could be observed (Fig. 3c). These results show that MnSe2 will be converted to α-MnSe under given reaction temperature and time, which can be attributed that NaCl-structured α-MnSe is more stable than MnSe2 with pyrite structure [23]. According to the literature [23], SeO2 could be deoxidized by hydrazine to Se quickly upon heating, which had high reactivity and was easy to be disproportionated into Se2− ions under alkaline conditions (Eqs. (1) and (2)). Free Mn2+ ions could react with Se2− ions to form MnSe (Eq. (3)). Glycerol can act as a complexing reagent to react with free Mn2+ ions to form a complex, which can affect the crystal growth process and decrease the particle size. $$SeO_2 + N_2H_4 \rightarrow Se \downarrow + N_2 \uparrow + 2H_2O \tag{1}$$ $$3Se + 6OH^- \rightarrow 2Se^{2-} + SeO_3^{2-} + 3H_2O \tag{2}$$ $$Mn^{2+} + Se^{2-} \rightarrow MnSe \downarrow \tag{3}$$ ![Fig. 3. XRD patterns of the synthesized MnSe samples with SDS and glycerol at 180 °C for different hydrothermal times (a: MnSe-9; b: MnSe-10; c: MnSe-4; d: MnSe-11).](figure3.png) ![Fig. 4. Dependence of the absorbance at 652 nm on the starting raw materials of the synthesized α-MnSe crystals (a: MnSe-1; b: MnSe-2; c: MnSe-3; d: MnSe-4; e: MnSe-5; f: MnSe-6; g: MnSe-7; h: MnSe-8; inset: corresponding color change catalyzed by MnSe-4).](figure4.png) #### 3.2. Peroxidase-like activity of α-MnSe TMB is a typical peroxidase substrate because it is colorless and is oxidized by H2O2 very slowly. Natural peroxidase can catalyze the oxidation of TMB substrate in the presence of H2O2 by one electron transfer and the consequent color change of the TMB oxidation product can be monitored from absorbance augmentation of the TMB oxidation product at 652 nm [1,2]. Fig. 4 shows that the synthesized α-MnSe crystals could catalyze the oxidation of TMB substrate with H2O2 and produced a nice blue color (the inset of Fig. 4). Fig. 4 also shows that the peroxidase-like activities of the resultant α-MnSe crystals varied with the starting raw materials. The MnSe-4 sample exhibited the highest catalytic activity, which can be attributed to its smallest particle size and special rough crystal morphology. These advantages may have more reaction active sizes, such that H2O2 and TMB can be easily adsorbed onto the sample surface to benefit the reaction. The peroxidase-like activity of the MnSe-4 sample was further investigated by steady-state kinetic experiments and the analysis results are shown in Fig. 5. As shown, by plotting the initial reaction rate against concentration, the typical Michaelis–Menten curves for TMB and H2O2 could be obtained in Fig. 5A and B. $K_m$ was obtained by using Lineweaver–Burk plots (Fig. 5C and D). The $K_m$ value for α-MnSe crystals with TMB as the substrate was 0.0348 mM, which was much lower than the reported value of horseradish peroxidase (HRP, 0.434 mM) [4], suggesting that α-MnSe crystals had higher affinity for TMB than HRP. On the other hand, the $K_m$ value for α-MnSe crystals with H2O2 as the substrate was 87 mM, significantly higher than that of 3.7 mM for HRP [4], suggesting that α-MnSe crystals had lower affinity for H2O2 than HRP. ![Fig. 5. Steady-state kinetic analysis and catalytic mechanism of the MnSe-4 sample: (A) the concentration of H2O2 is 0.2 M and TMB concentration was varied; (B) the TMB concentration is 0.5 mM and the concentration of H2O2 was varied; (C and D) double reciprocal plots of activity of the MnSe-4 sample with the concentration of one substrate (TMB or H2O2) fixed and the other varied. The error bars represent the standard error derived from three measurements.](figure5.png) ![Fig. 6. Ten-cycling optical detection of TMB liquid substrate system using the MnSe-4 sample as the peroxidase catalyst.](figure6.png) As an inorganic enzyme mimic, α-MnSe may exhibit high chemical and thermal stability. To investigate this advantage, recycling experiments were performed, and peroxidase-like activity was monitored on the basis of its absorbance at 652 nm in each cycle. The cycle performance of the MnSe-4 sample is shown in Fig. 6. The results reveal no clear loss of activity was observed, even after 10 cycles, suggesting that as a crystal-based peroxidase mimic, α-MnSe crystals have two potential advantages, including (1) stable activity and (2) ease of recovery and reuse. After 10 cycles, the sample was reclaimed and analyzed by XRD and SEM. The results are shown in Figs. 7 and 8, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7, all characteristic diffraction peaks could be indexed to the α-MnSe phase. The SEM image reveals that the morphology of the reclaimed sample was similar with that of the original sample before 10 cycles (Fig. 8). These results provide strong evidence that α-MnSe crystals possess excellent robustness, stable activity, and good reusability. ![Fig. 7. XRD pattern of the MnSe-4 sample reclaimed (a) before and (b) after 10 cycles.](figure7.png) ![Fig. 8. SEM image of the MnSe-4 sample reclaimed (a) before and (b) after 10 cycles.](figure8.png) ### 4. Conclusions In summary, α-MnSe crystals have been prepared through a facile hydrothermal process in a glycerol/water solution system using SeO2 as selenium source and hydrous hydrazine as reducing agent. Their peroxidase-like activity was first demonstrated. The particle sizes and morphologies of the resulting samples could be tailored by adjusting the starting raw materials. SDS and glycerol can help decrease the particle size of α-MnSe crystals to enhance the peroxidase-like activity. The steady-state kinetic analysis shows α-MnSe crystals had higher affinity for TMB and lower affinity for H2O2 compared with HRP. Moreover, α-MnSe peroxidase mimic had long-term stability (no activity loss after 10 cycles) and ease of recovery and reuse. Because this route is easily accessible, α-MnSe peroxidase mimic has great potential for practice use in chemical/bio detection.
